coredata.dc:title: A new insight on the role of CQDs in driving BiOBr into broader-spectrum-response: Dual function of up-conversion and photosensitization effect
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Dong
Liu, Linlin
Wang, Yawen
Fan, Caimei
Huang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A carbon quantum dots modified bismuth bromide composite photocatalyst (CQDs/BiOBr) was synthesized by a mild hydrothermal method. The CQDs/BiOBr exhibited enhanced photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A. The reason could be attributed to the broader light respond region and enhanced separation ratio of photo-induced electron-hole pairs after the introducing of CQDs. Interestingly, not only the up-conversion photoluminescence property, but also the photosensitization effect of CQDs worked together in driving BiOBr into broader-spectrum-response.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
BiOBr
Broad-spectrum-response
Photosensitization
Up-conversion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of samples, (c) Left: TEM image of CQDs, right: TEM image of CQDs/BiOBr, (d) HRTEM image of CQDs/BiOBr composite.
Photocatalytic activity of BPA under (a) visible light and (b) different monochromatic lights irradiation.

(a) PL spectra of CQDs; (b) DRS spectra, radical trapping experiment under monochromatic light irradiation; (c) BiOBr at 365 nm; (d) CQDs/BiOBr at 365 nm; (e) CQDs/BiOBr at 590 nm and (f) schematic illustration for the photocatalytic process.

head.ce:title: A new insight on the role of CQDs in driving BiOBr into broader-spectrum-response: Dual function of up-conversion and photosensitization effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan 030008, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan 030008, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A carbon quantum dots modified bismuth bromide composite photocatalyst (CQDs/BiOBr) was synthesized by a mild hydrothermal method. The CQDs/BiOBr exhibited enhanced photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A. The reason could be attributed to the broader light respond region and enhanced separation ratio of photo-induced electron-hole pairs after the introducing of CQDs. Interestingly, not only the up-conversion photoluminescence property, but also the photosensitization effect of CQDs worked together in driving BiOBr into broader-spectrum-response.

ce:section.ce:para: BiOBr is a promising photocatalyst owing to the suitable band gap and environmental-friendly qualities [1]. However, its photocatalytic activity still limited by the low utilization rate of sunlight and high ratio of photo-induced electron-hole recombination. To change this situation, many approaches have been applied to change that, such as doping with ions, constructing heterojunction, introducing oxygen vacancies and modification with noble metals [2–5]. In addition to these, compositing with CQDs has been proved an effective way by broadening the light response region of BiOBr and increasing the separation ratio of photo-induced carriers [6–9]. CQDs as a novel nanocarbon materials (less than 10 nm) have displayed many excellent properties, such as photoluminescence performance, ultraviolet–visible absorption, and unique up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) property, and have been applied in numerous fields such as sensors, bioimaging, photocatalysis and light-emitting devices [10–13]. To CQDs/BiOBr photocatalyst, many reports have proved UCPL property of CQDs played important role in broadening the light response region of BiOBr, where CQDs could transfer long wavelength light to short wavelength light to active photocatalyst in visible even near-infrared region [8,14,15]. However, CQDs have a wide range of light responses, which could make CQDs exciting and then transferring photo-excited electrons to photocatalysts, finally resulting in an effective photoactivity, just like a photosensitizer [16,17]. Yu et al. reported the photocatalytic H2 production behavior of CQDs/P25 in visible light could be attributed to the photosensitization effect, but not the UCPL effect of CQDs [18]. Therefore, there are more possibilities in the role of CQDs in CQDs/BiOBr composite.

Here we synthesized a CQDs/BiOBr composite and deeply studied the role of CQDs. The photocatalytic mechanism could ascribe to the broadening light respond region and enhancing photo-induced electron–hole pairs separation efficiency by introducing CQDs as reported previously. While interestingly, not only the up-conversion effect, but also the photosensitization effect of CQDs worked together in driving BiOBr into broader-spectrum-response.

CQDs were prepared via the hydrothermal reactions in a Teflon-sealed autoclave at 160 °C for 6 h by mixing 0.5 g of urea and 1.0 g of ascorbic acid in 20 mL of distilled water. After cooling to room temperature, larger particles were removed by filtered and centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 10 min) the obtained brown syrup mixture. Then the solution was further dialyzed using dialysis membrane with retained molecular weight 3000 Da for 2 days to remove impurities. Finally, the CQDs solution with brown color was obtained for further using. To prepare CQDs/BiOBr, KBr (4 mmol) and Bi(NO)3‧5H2O (4 mmol) were added in 20 mL water followed by adding 4 mL CQDs solution. The pH value was adjusted to 13.2 by 2 M NaOH with continuous stirring. Then the solution was hydrothermal heated at 160 °C for 16 h. Finally, the resultant precipitate was washed two times by distilled water and then dried in air. Pure BiOBr was synthesized by the same process just without CQDs solution added.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of samples were characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku D/max-2500 diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was performed on a FT/IR-4200, Jasco international, Co., Ltd. using KBr disk as the reference. UV–Vis Diffused Reflectance Spectrum (UV–Vis DRS) of the samples were performed on an UV–visible spectrometer (Lambda 600, PerkinElmer, USA). The microscopic structure information was performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-2010, JEOL, Japan). The up-conversion photoluminescence property (UCPL) was characterized by photoluminescence (PerkinElmer Instruments LS-55) spectrum (Xe lamp as excitation source).

The photocatalytic activities of the samples were tested by degradation of bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light (A 500 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm filter (AC/250 V, Shanghai, LanSheng electronics co., LTD,) and different monochromatic light (3 W, Shenzhen lamplic co., LTD.) irradiation. 0.1 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in 15 mg/L BPA solution with 100 mL volume in a typical test. The adsorption-desorption equilibrium was achieved after stirred 20 min in dark. Then the test was beginning with the light on under stirring. 4 mL of suspension was centrifuged and then tested by a UV–vis spectrometer (Varian Cary 50 prode) at given time intervals. The degradation efficiency of BPA was calculated by (1 − C/C0) × 100%, where C is the concentration of BPA and C0 is the initial concentration at a certain time.

The XRD patterns of samples were shown in Fig. 1 a. The characteristic diffraction peaks of both samples could index to normative tetragonal BiOBr (JCPDS No. 9-393). Introducing CQDs did not change the structure of BiOBr. The FT-IR spectra of samples was shown in Fig. 1b. For CQDs/BiOBr, all the characteristic peaks of CQDs and BiOBr were found. And there were hydrophilic function groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl) covered on the surface of BiOBr after introducing CQDs according to the existing of peak at 847 cm−1 (belong to skeletal vibration of sp3 COH) and peaks at 1496, 1620 and 3379 cm−1 (assign to the stretching vibrations of COO, CO and OH, respectively) [19–21]. TEM image of CQDs and CQDs/BiOBr, and HRTEM image of CQDs/BiOBr composite were shown in Fig. 1c and d. In CQDs/BiOBr, CQDs nanoparticles with diameter about 5~9 nm were well dispersed on the sheets of BiOBr.

The photocatalytic performance was evaluated through photodegradation of bisphenol A (BPA). For pure BiOBr, 11.5% of BPA was decomposed (Fig. 2 a) under visible light irradiation. The efficiency was greatly promoted and almost 67.9% of BPA was removed after the introduction of CQDs. The degradation efficiency of CQDs/BiOBr declined about 5.3% after 4 cycles (Fig. S1), showed a relative high durability. We then studied the photocatalytic activity under different monochromatic light irradiation. The pure BiOBr had effective efficiency of degrading BPA under 365 nm light irradiation (Fig. 2b), and which was slightly improved by the introduction of CQDs. Under monochromatic light of 590 and even 850 nm irradiation, CQDs/BiOBr composite still showed effective photocatalytic efficiency, while BiOBr had almost no effect. The above results indicated CQDs/BiOBr had a wide responding region in light.

To detect the roles of CQDs, a series of characters were tested as follows. The UCPL property of CQDs was detected as shown in Fig. 3 a. CQDs exhibited UCPL emissions in 300~500 nm when it was excited by light with wavelengths ranging from 600~900 nm, exhibiting up-conversion photoluminescence property.

Fig. 3b showed the DRS spectra, in which the CQDs/BiOBr composite exhibited enhanced light absorption intensity in the range of wavelengths greater than 400 nm, and the absorption edge slightly appeared red shift compared to pure BiOBr owing to the interaction between CQDs and BiOBr [22]. This result indicated BiOBr had a wide range of light responses after introducing of CQDs.

Then, we detected the oxidative species by adding different scavengers (isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for
OH, ammonium oxalate (AO) for h+ and AgNO3 for e−, bubbling N2 was used to prevent the formation of
O2 −). As shown in Fig. 3c, under 365 nm light irradiation, for pure BiOBr, h+ played a leading role,
OH, e− and
O2 − were completely irrelevant. When introducing CQDs on BiOBr, as shown in Fig. 3d, e− and
O2 − still not worked at all at UV light irradiation, but
OH combined with h+ played synergistic effect. The reason for this change could be ascribed to the reference of carboxyl and hydroxyl brought by the CQDs (according to the FT-IR analysis), which was beneficial to adsorb OH− to generate
OH with h+ [19,23]. Then as shown in Fig. 3e, under 590 nm light irradiation for CQDs/BiOBr, h+ and
OH still had effect, interestingly, e− and
O2 − started to work. The ESR results under 590 ± 5 nm light irradiation (Fig. S2a and b) also showed both
O2 − and
OH were clearly detected for CQDs/BiOBr, further demonstrated both e− and h+ played a role in photodegradation of BPA under 590 nm light irradiation. This result is interesting. Under such long wavelength light irradiation, BiOBr should not exhibit any effective photocatalytic activity. The only reason should be attributed to the introduction of CQDs. Since e− did not work for BiOBr in UV light, then in visible light, whether the CQD as a photosensitizer or up-conversion phosphor could be determined by the active oxidative species. In the light wavelength range where the BiOBr could not be excited, if the photocatalytic activity was generated by acting CQDs as a photosensitizer, e− should play a leading role other than h+. On the other side, if the performance was caused by the UCPL property of CQDs, then the e− should not work at all according to the silence of e− under UV light irradiation (Fig. 3c and d). Therefore, the only reasonable explanation of the result in Fig. 3e, was the CQDs played dual function. One was acting as up-conversion materials to excite the photocatalyst in the irradiation of longer wavelength light. Another one was acting as a photosensitizer to inject e− to BiOBr, followed by reacting with oxygen molecule to produce
O2 − (as shown in Fig. 3f). The results in Fig. S2c further demonstrated this conclusion. At whole visible-light region, h+,
OH, e− and
O2 − were all taken part in.

In summary, we found up-conversion and photosensitization of CQDs could play synergistic effect to drive BiOBr into broader-spectrum-response on the CQDs/BiOBr photocatalytic composite. Overall, this work could promote our in-depth thinking on the interaction between the CQDs and photocatalysts.

Dong Wang: Writing - original draft. Linlin Liu: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Yawen Wang: Writing - review & editing, Investigation. Caimei Fan: Writing - review & editing, Investigation. Wei Huang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 21303116, 21676178).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2020.137340.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Figs. S1 and S2
